2
Crop Section

Drag a rectangle around the cross section. After applying, the image becomes a faded backdrop you can trace over. Areas outside the crop become white so you can extend lines anywhere.

Don't need a crop? Use the full image as-is:

3
Set Scale

Click two known points (Bluebeam-style). Hold ⇧ on the second click to lock to horizontal/vertical. The full page is visible here so you can pick references outside your crop area; the dashed blue box shows the crop bounds.

Points: 0 / 2
Real distance (m)
Anamorphic scan? (different horizontal & vertical scales)

Tick this if your drawing was issued with a vertical exaggeration (e.g. 1:100 H × 1:50 V long-section). Provide TWO known distances — one between two horizontal points and one between two vertical points. SCOPE will rescale the image to 1:1 so the in-app workspace is true metres in both axes.

1. Pick two horizontal reference points on the canvas, then enter the real horizontal distance:

H pts
0 / 2
Real H (m)

2. Pick two vertical reference points (typically the same depth on two side-by-side scans, or two RL grid lines), then enter the real vertical distance:

V pts
0 / 2
Real V (m)

After rescaling, downstream steps (Trace, Materials, etc.) work in the corrected workspace and a single uniform scale is in force.

4
Trace & Edit
Drawing Style

Sets what kind of object newly drawn lines represent — drives the default colour, dash style, and PLAXIS export semantics. Existing elements aren't affected; change them via the Type dropdown in the Elements list.

Width 2 px
Elements 0

Per-element type sits in each row's Type: dropdown above. Bulk: Ctrl+A select all · Del delete · Ctrl+C/V copy/paste — see Shortcuts below.

Region Operations

Available after Step 5 — Detect regions. Toggles are sticky: stay in mode and operate on multiple regions, then toggle off when done.

Model Domain

Lowers the bottom of the model. Every vertex on the current bottom boundary slides to the new bottom y — corners translate straight down; vertices where a dipping stratum boundary lands extend along that dip (the line from the adjacent vertex is extended until it hits the new floor). The model extends laterally to wherever the dip vectors land — the chainage axis grows if necessary. Walls / reinforcement / loads stay in place. Or drag the dashed bottom edge on the canvas.

Extend base by (m)

Move Selected (m)

Move selected elements precisely. Or use arrow keys (1×grid spacing).

dX (Ch)
dY (RL)
 
Boreholes

Pick the Borehole tool from the canvas toolbar (or press B), click the collar location, then drag down to set depth (or shift-drag for vertical lock). Edit layer markers via the Edit button below. To hide all boreholes on the canvas, use the toggle in the View panel.

Markup 0

Drawing aids only — not exported to PLAXIS and ignored during region detection. Use the M tool for rulers (2 clicks → labelled distance), the T tool for text annotations (click → type), and the A tool for arrows (2 clicks: tail → tip). In Select, click any markup to pick it up; drag to move; Del to remove.

Keyboard shortcuts
Vselect   Qlasso   Ppolyline   Rrect   Bborehole
Mruler (markup)   Ttext annotation (markup)   Aarrow (markup)
Ctrl+Aselect all   Del/⌫delete selected (or undo last vertex while drawing)
Ctrl+Ccopy   Ctrl+Vpaste   Ctrl+Zundo   Ctrl+Yredo
⇧ortho-lock   Spacepan   →↑↓←nudge
Right-clickinsert/del vertex   Esccancel
5
Materials, Regions & Surcharges
Materials 0

Define soil and rock unit properties up front, then assign each detected region to a material. Mohr-Coulomb is the default; switch to Hoek-Brown for rock or Linear Elastic for very stiff inclusions. Properties feed straight into the PLAXIS export pipeline.

Materials summary
Regions —

Define every soil polygon needed for the analysis here. Per-stage activation is set in Step 7 — Construction Stages.

⚠ Elements have changed since detection — re-run to refresh.

Loads 0

Set the magnitude / direction for each Surcharge / load line (uniform or trapezoidal kPa, σx / σy) and each Point load (concentrated Fx / Fy in kN) drawn in Step 4. Per-stage activation is set in Step 7 — Construction Stages.

6
Structures & ML Optimisation

Two independent workflows. Fill out either, both, or neither. Each section has its own download button — the ML notebook ignores the manual list and vice versa, so a saved manual layout doesn't get lost when you also want to run an ML sweep.

Manual structural elements —

Assign a PLAXIS structural type (and its parameters) to each Wall / sheet pile or Reinforcement line drawn in Step 4. Defaults follow common AS / Eurocode practice and can be overridden per element.

How do I model nails / piles fixed to a shotcrete facing?

Pattern: draw the shotcrete as a Wall / sheet pile polyline along the cut face, then draw each nail / pile as a Reinforcement line whose head terminates exactly on the shotcrete polyline. SCOPE's vertex glue automatically merges the nail-head vertex with the polygon Point underlying the shotcrete, so PLAXIS sees them as connected — no extra step required. In Step 6, set the wall's structural kind to Shotcrete facing (the new option) and each nail to Soil nail / Pile. The structural-row card auto-fills EA / EI from d, Econc, and γconc.

Watch out: the shotcrete polyline must be drawn as a single piece touching every nail head. If you draw the shotcrete in segments and a nail head lands between segment endpoints, the glue won't fire and PLAXIS will see two separate elements.

Walls 0
Reinforcement 0

All values are taken as PLAXIS 2D plane-strain inputs (per metre out of plane). Activation per stage and pre-stress per stage are configured in Step 7.

Deterministic PLAXIS notebook — uses exactly the walls / reinforcement listed above plus the regions, materials, surcharges, and stages from Steps 5 / 7. Download from Save / Export.

ML-optimised reinforcement —

Declare a search space and one or more reinforcement categories. The exported notebook runs Bayesian active-learning and reports the cheapest layout meeting the FoS target.

tick to include in ML search

Adds a wall category to the ML search. Pick the installation line (cyan), declare embedment / lateral-position bounds, and pick the wall section (one per export — re-export to compare types).

Search bounds

Lembed = wall length below the picked installation line · wall_x = horizontal x-coordinate along the picked line where the wall is drilled (auto-fitted to the line's x-range) · sinplane = out-of-plane c–c spacing.

Section catalog

Forces compare mode — adds a virtual section that runs nails-only. Requires nails enabled. Useful when you want the optimiser to tell you whether the wall is even needed.

Wall installation line not picked

Click a polyline on the canvas — the wall extends from this line downward by Lembed.

8-D nail dims

Per-row variable length (L1..Ln), spacing, inclination, top-row position, row pitch Δy. Maximum row count is a cap — the optimiser may use fewer rows if Δy lands on a larger value within the allowed band.

Search bounds

Lmin/Lmax apply to every row independently. s = out-of-plane c-c spacing. θ = inclination below horizontal. Δy = vertical row pitch (typical engineering practice 1.5–2.5 m).

Row layout

The optimiser samples Δy in [Δymin, Δymax] and uses up to this many rows — fewer if Δy lands large. Each enabled length-profile family runs as its own GP. Total nail-block dims per family shown below.

Length-profile families 3 enabled

Each ticked family runs as its own GP. The optimiser will rank all configurations across (family × wall section) and report the cheapest meeting the FoS target. Default all-on lets the data tell you which family wins.

Fraction of n_active rows that fall into the upper tier (rest are lower tier). Default 0.2–0.8 avoids degenerate splits at 0 or 1.

Nail geometry & cost

Stock-length step: snaps each best Li UP to the nearest multiple at deliverable stage (typical drill-rod 3 m, 0 = no snap). Per-row setup cost: nail-equivalent metres added per active row in the cost function — rewards the optimiser for using fewer rows when they suffice (typical 2–4 m, 0 = pure ΣL).

Nailable face not picked

Click a polyline on the canvas — every trial nail-row anchors to this line and projects outward at angle θ. The picked face is the search range; rows can be placed anywhere along it. The reported "top-row offset" is measured from the top of this picked polyline, not from the slope crest. If you want a crest setback (~0.5–1 m, tension-crack zone) or a toe setback (~0.3–0.5 m, low overburden / drill-rig bench), pick the polyline starting below the crest and finishing above the toe — the optimiser cannot see the crest itself.

Tip: click vertices, double-click / Enter to commit, Esc to cancel. Bounds auto-fit on commit — override above if needed.

requires Wall

Models a pre-stressed ground anchor (tieback) attached to the wall top — the classic anchored-wall pattern. Near end pinned to the wall plate, far end fixed in soil at inclination θanc, with a locked-off prestress force applied during wall installation.

Why "requires Wall": the near end attaches to the wall plate — without a wall there's nothing to react against. For a strut between two faces or a standalone ground anchor (no wall), use the manual structural elements list above instead. PLAXIS-side this emits as a NodeToNodeAnchor.

Search bounds

L = total anchor length (free + bond) · Fp = locked-off prestress force per anchor · sanc = out-of-plane anchor c–c.

Anchor geometry

Anchor inclined below horizontal at this angle. Free length 70%, bond length 30% (typical).

▾ Include from Manual static fixtures across every trial

Tick a Manual wall / reinforcement to fix it across every ML trial. Use for a shotcrete facing the optimised nails attach to, a pre-installed wall, an as-built test nail row, etc. Surcharges, point loads and water tables are always passed through (ground-model boundary conditions — no toggle).

No Manual walls or reinforcement drawn in Step 4. To include a shotcrete facing or static wall here, draw it as a Wall / sheet pile polyline and assign its structural kind in the Manual structural elements section above.

▾ Objective & budget global

Applies across all active categories.

Total PLAXIS runs = initial + active. Each run takes ~15–30 s.

Active-learning notebook — Bayesian search with a Gaussian-Process surrogate. Independent of the Manual section above. Download from Save / Export.

7
Construction Stages

Define construction stages (PLAXIS phases). Each stage inherits from its parent — only changes are stored. Click a stage to edit it; tick / untick / clear (◯ = inherit) per element.

Stages 0
Stage editor —

Select a stage above to edit it.

When you're done, use Save / Export → PLAXIS Python in the toolbar to generate the script.

Output / Reporting

What the emitted notebook reports after Calculate. Lists below auto-rebuild from your stages (Step 7) and structures (Step 6) — open this panel after any change to refresh.

Output folder
PNG export size (px)
Summary table summary.csv + console
Contour plots PLAXIS-native PNG

Add stages in Step 7 to populate this matrix.

Click a column header to apply that result-type to every phase. Click a row label to apply that phase across every result-type.

Structural plots auto from Step 6

Add walls / reinforcement in Step 6 to populate this list.

Format:

"PLAXIS-native" looks just like the screen output in PLAXIS. The "Python plot" version is a standalone matplotlib figure designed for inclusion in a written report — same data, cleaner styling.

FoS curves Sum-Msf vs |u|

Curve point: auto — node of max displacement at each Safety step. Canvas pick is not yet wired.

Settlement profile Uy along a drawn line

Draw the line(s) on the section with the “Settlement query line (output)” category (object-category picker), e.g. a horizontal line just under the footing. The exporter samples vertical displacement (Uy) along it and emits a settlement-vs-chainage PNG + CSV with the peak annotated, for each stage ticked below.

Mesh density PLAXIS Mesh.Coarseness

Drives g_i.Model.MeshCoarseness in the emitted Python notebook and is reported in the input-summary PDF.

Advanced

All Step 8 settings persist with the session JSON. Reporting cells appear at the end of the emitted Python notebook (cells 10+).

Chainage (m)

Reference dots you can drop anywhere and pin to an exact Chainage / RL — type the values or paste a list. The Polyline tool snaps to them, and the whole layer can be hidden once you're done. Drawing aids only — never exported.

Grid & Axes

Original colour helps when the source uses colour-coded materials (e.g. SLOPE/W). Show-uncropped temporarily reveals everything beyond the crop boundary so you can reference material tables or surrounding details — drawing tools still work only inside the crop.

Overlay Images

Add one or more secondary images (borehole figures, adjacent pages of a long section, etc.) and align each via two reference points. For multi-page stitching, load page 2, align it to page 1; then load page 3, align it to the combined page-1+2 scene; and so on.

Import an image, PDF, or saved session to begin. 100%
Session
Notes

Reload by dropping the file on the import area in Step 1.

Export drawing
PLAXIS
Notes

Generates a Jupyter notebook for the PLAXIS Python 3 kernel (s_i / g_i preloaded). Cells: imports → project setup → materials → polygons → structures → mesh → stages → calculate. Uses the Manual structural elements list from Step 6.

Notes

Active-learning notebook: Bayesian search over the bounds you declare in Step 6 → ML-optimised reinforcement, with a Gaussian Process surrogate. Per-row variable nail length plus optional Wall and Anchor categories. Requires at least one ML sub-category ticked with its face / installation line picked.

5 seeds
Notes

PowerShell orchestrator for the Mode-2 notebook above. Launches PLAXIS headless, queues N seeds back-to-back, auto-resumes after PLAXIS hangs, moves outputs into Plaxis Results/seed<N>/. Save next to the .ipynb (any folder), then run: .\run_seeds.ps1 (or pass -Seeds 7,8,9 to override the baked-in default).

GeoStudio SLOPE/W (multi-stage)
Enabled stages
— refresh menu after editing Step 7 —

Tick “Include in SLOPE/W (.gsz) export” on each stage in Step 7. Entry/exit slip-search segments are auto-derived from the active ground surface (drag handles + custom interval counts coming in 5B).

RF = 1 / (φg · φn) = 2.857
Notes

Bond-stress reduction factor for reinforcement, emitted as <PulloutReductionFactor>. GeoStudio divides the unfactored bond stress by this value (Factored = Unfactored / RF). Defaults: φ_g = 0.5 (TfNSW geotechnical strength reduction), φ_n = 0.7 (importance category) → RF ≈ 2.857.

Notes

Reinforcement table + seismic kh label (when used) draw on the plot in both views. Title block + border render in the Page Layout / print view only (not the Define view, which opens first) — date, analysis name and scale auto-fill on open; the title comes from Project Info. Markup passthrough places SCOPE annotation text (and construction lines) onto the plot.

Notes

Emits a Morgenstern-Price single-analysis .gsz consuming Mode-1 layout: regions → soils, soil nails + ground anchors → reinforcement, surcharges → line loads, water table → piezometric surface. Limit-equilibrium: FEM-only models (HS / SS / HB) collapse to Mohr-Coulomb strength; walls / point loads are not emitted. Open in GeoStudio 25.x and click Solve.

Report
Notes

Engineering appendix for the PLAXIS model — project metadata, traced cross-section, materials, structural elements with calculation workings, and per-stage detail blocks. The author from Project Info is used in the sign-off block.

Notes

Tutorial-style hand-check for traced gravity / cantilever walls — geometry, earth-pressure coefficients (spelled out), per-stage active-pressure diagrams decomposed into labelled regions, force/moment tables and the sliding / overturning / bearing checks (UNFACTORED). Mononobe-Okabe for seismic stages. Requires a wall drawn with the "Gravity wall outline (stability check)" style.

Workflow inspector

Borehole Layers

Each row below is a stratigraphic interface depth (m from collar). The label is the material below that interface, displayed centred in its depth range. Use the surface field for the material from 0 m down to the first interface. Rows are re-ordered by depth automatically on Save, so you can enter them in any order; use a row's + to insert a forgotten intermediate layer directly below it.

Total depth (m)
Collar RL (m)
Inclination: — from horizontal.

Surface material (0 m → first interface)
Interface depth (m)

Add Overlay Image

Name
PDF page
/ —

Drag to define a crop region (e.g. trim title block, legend or margins). Wheel-zoom and space-drag (or middle-click drag) to navigate large pages. If no crop is drawn, the whole page/image is added.

No crop drawn — whole page will be used.

Material

Mohr-Coulomb is the default for soils and for drained rock characterised by c′/φ′. Switch to Hoek-Brown for rock units characterised by σci/GSI/mi, or Linear Elastic for very stiff rock / rigid inclusions where no failure is expected. The primary subtype is what you'll use most often; optionally add an alternate subtype so the same material can be applied across different analysis stages (Mohr-Coulomb only). On PLAXIS export each active subtype emits as a separate material record.

Name *
Colour *
Material model *
Primary drainage subtype *

Pick the subtype that matches the loading rate vs the soil's drainage path. Rule of thumb: low-permeability soils + fast loading → undrained; high-permeability soils OR slow / steady-state loading → drained.

Subtype Strength input Use when…
Drainedc′, φ′Sands / gravels (any speed); long-term clay; rock; embankment fills after consolidation. e.g. highway slope under self-weight, FoS at 50 yr design life.
Undrained (A)c′, φ′Saturated low-perm soil under fast loading where pwp evolves. e.g. rapid embankment construction on soft clay; staged loading + Consolidation phases between.
Undrained (B)cu, φ=0Short-term total-stress check with a measured / interpreted cu profile. e.g. end-of-construction stability of a clay slope using AS 3798 / Eurocode-7 short-term values.
Undrained (C)cu, φ=0Same as (B) but no pwp generated — fastest. Pick when you don't plan a Consolidation phase. e.g. single-stage Safety FoS check with no time-dependent dissipation.
Non-porousc′, φ′No pore water at all. e.g. mass concrete plug, dry rock above the water table treated as fully drained, sealed structural fills.

Tip: hover any option in the dropdown for a one-line reminder. For the same material across stages (e.g. clay short-term vs long-term), tick "Also define an alternate subtype" below — both subtypes export as separate PLAXIS materials so the Construction Stages step can swap them per phase.

γ — unit weight (kN/m³) *
c′ — effective cohesion (kPa) *
φ′ — effective friction angle (°) *
E — Young's modulus (kPa) *
ν — Poisson's ratio *
cu — undrained shear strength (kPa) * [φ implicitly 0 for this subtype]
γ — unit weight (kN/m³) *
σci — intact UCS (kPa) *
GSI — Geological Strength Index *
mi — Hoek-Brown constant *
D — disturbance factor *
Erm — rock mass modulus (kPa)
ν — Poisson's ratio *

PLAXIS uses these to compute the equivalent rock-mass parameters internally. Equivalent c′, φ′ are derived per-element from the local minor principal stress σ3 via the Hoek-Brown failure envelope; you don't need to supply them. If Erm is left blank, PLAXIS uses the Hoek-Diederichs (2006) formula. Drainage is generally treated as Drained for rock — alternate subtypes are not offered.

γ — unit weight (kN/m³) *
E — Young's modulus (kPa) *
ν — Poisson's ratio *

Linear elastic = no failure criterion. Use only for very stiff rock or rigid inclusions where you're confident yielding won't occur. PLAXIS will not check strength against any limit.

γunsat — unit weight (kN/m³) *
γsat — saturated unit weight (kN/m³)
E50ref — secant stiffness at 50% qf (kPa) *
Eoedref — oedometer tangent stiffness (kPa) *
Eurref — unload/reload stiffness (kPa) *
m — power for stress dependency *
νur — unload/reload Poisson's *
c′ — effective cohesion (kPa) *
φ′ — effective friction angle (°) *
ψ — dilatancy angle (°)
pref — reference stress (kPa)

Initial-stress / interface / permeability parameters (OCR, POP, Rinter, σt, K0, kx, ky) are now in the Common (initial state & interface) section below — shared with Mohr-Coulomb so the same field exists in one place.

HS uses three reference stiffnesses at pref: E50ref (deviatoric loading), Eoedref (1-D compression), Eurref (unload/reload). Stress-dependent stiffness scales as (σ′/pref)m. m≈0.5 for sand, ≈1.0 for soft clay. Drainage type drives undrained excess-pore-pressure generation; HS's stress-state-dependent shear cap is what lets undrained Safety FoS differ from drained.

γunsat — unit weight (kN/m³) *
γsat — saturated unit weight (kN/m³)
λ* — modified compression index *

λ* = λ / (1 + e₀) — slope of the e–ln p′ NCL.

κ* — modified swelling index *

κ* = κ / (1 + e₀) — slope of the unload/reload line. Usually κ* ≈ λ*/5 to λ*/10.

νur — unload/reload Poisson's *
c′ — effective cohesion (kPa) *
φ′ — effective friction angle (°) *
ψ — dilatancy angle (°)

Soft Soil is a critical-state model for soft, normally-consolidated clays. Logarithmic compression (slope λ*) and swelling (slope κ*) — better for primary settlement than MC, lighter than HS. Strength still governed by c′/φ′. Pre-consolidation: set OCR or POP in the Common section. Drainage typically Undrained (A); pair with the Consolidation phase type (Step 7) to model excess-pore-pressure dissipation over time.

M and K₀NC are not editable here — PLAXIS computes both from φ′ (K₀NC = 1 − sin(φ′)) and rejects any attempt to override them via the Python API.

Alternate subtype

On export, alternate emits as — with a shaded version of the colour

MC stiffness / strength gradients (advanced)

Empty = use the default formula shown in the placeholder. These fields are Mohr-Coulomb-specific (HS handles stress-dependent stiffness via the m exponent on E50ref).

ψ — dilatancy (°)
γsat — saturated unit weight (kN/m³)
Einc — E gradient (kPa/m)
cinc — c gradient (kPa/m)
yref — reference RL/Y for Einc, cinc (m)

Flags this material as a flow barrier (concrete wall, sheet pile, shotcrete). Auto-ticked for concrete / linear-elastic / "wall"-named materials; untick if this layer is actually permeable. Carried into the Mode-2 (ML) export as a material attribute. NOTE: the Mode-1 export is now always hydrostatic — steady-state seepage was removed, so this flag no longer changes Mode-1 pore pressures.

Common (initial state & interface)

Initial-stress, interface and permeability parameters. Apply to Mohr-Coulomb and Hardening Soil. Leave blank = PLAXIS default.

Rinter — interface strength reduction

Reduces wall-soil friction (and adhesion) at PosInterface / NegInterface lines. PLAXIS sets τinterface = Rinter × τsoil.

K₀ — earth pressure at rest
OCR — over-consolidation ratio
POP — pre-overburden pressure (kPa)
σt — tension cut-off (kPa)

When set, emits TensionCutOff=True with this tensile-strength limit. Leave blank to keep PLAXIS default (no cap explicitly set).

kx — horizontal permeability (m/day)
ky — vertical permeability (m/day)
Reinforcement interface (optional)

Used by reinforcement elements (piles, soil nails, anchors, micropiles) when their bond mode is set to Auto from material. PLAXIS Tskin = τbond × π × Dhole, length-weighted across crossed materials.

τbond — ultimate bond stress (kPa)
TfNSW indicative ultimate bond stress ranges (click to expand)

Soil — ultimate τbond:

  • Engineered fill (poorly compacted): ~20 kPa
  • Engineered fill (well compacted): ~30 kPa
  • Residual soil — Firm (F): ~20 kPa
  • Residual soil — Stiff (St): 20–50 kPa
  • Residual soil — Very stiff (VSt): 40–80 kPa
  • Residual soil — Hard (H): 50–75 kPa

Rock (Sydney basin) — ultimate τbond:

  • Sandstone Class I–II: 2000–3000 kPa (allowable ~1000–1500 kPa)
  • Sandstone Class III: 1000–1800 kPa
  • Sandstone Class IV: 400–1000 kPa
  • Sandstone Class V: 200–300 kPa
  • Shale Class I: 1200–2000 kPa
  • Shale Class II: 900–2000 kPa
  • Shale Class III: 600–900 kPa
  • Shale Class IV: ~300 kPa
  • Shale Class V: 150–200 kPa
  • Dyke / weathered intrusive: 250–500 kPa

Source: TfNSW soil & rock anchor database (Northwest Rail Link, NorthConnex, M5 East, WestConnex M5/KGRI, M4–M5 Link Tunnels). Use as a starting estimate only — values vary with drilling method, hole diameter, grout pressure, and rock fabric. Verify with project-specific pull-out testing and reference AS 5100.3 / FHWA-IF-99-015 / Bustamante & Doix.

Import GeoStudio (.gsz)

—

Tick the analyses you want to import. Each becomes one SCOPE stage. Shared geometry (Points / Regions / Materials) is imported once. PLAXIS-only fields (HS/SS, plate EA/EI, anchor stiffness, mesh, phase types) will need to be filled in after import.

Warnings / unknown tags
—
